prompt: Research the current applications and recent scientific advancements of various light-based therapies (e.g., laser, IPL, LED) in aesthetic medicine for treating conditions such as photoaging, promoting skin whitening/brightening, and reducing hyperpigmentation (like age spots or melasma).
article:
## 1) What “light-based therapy” means in aesthetic dermatology (and why results differ by device)

Light-based treatments in aesthetic medicine generally fall into three mechanistic buckets:

1) **Targeted photothermolysis of chromophores** (melanin, hemoglobin, water) → lesion clearance (lentigines), vascular reduction (erythema/telangiectasia), controlled thermal injury (remodeling). This is the core logic of **lasers** and **IPL**. IPL is explicitly described as polychromatic, noncoherent light used for pigmented and vascular lesions by selecting wavelength bands, pulse structure and fluence to preferentially heat melanin/hemoglobin targets [[44]](https://ijdvl.com/content/126/2024/0/1/pdf/IJDVL_1029_2022.pdf).

2) **Fractional photothermolysis** (ablative or non‑ablative microcolumns) → microscopic treatment zones (MTZs) with surrounding viable tissue to speed healing and reduce downtime. This underpins **fractional CO₂ / Er:YAG**, **1550/1565 nm non‑ablative fractional**, and **1927 nm thulium fractional** devices [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12264580/).

3) **Photobiomodulation (PBM)** (often LED) → predominantly **non-thermal signaling modulation** (mitochondrial, inflammatory, vascular, melanogenesis pathways), aiming to change biology without intentional tissue destruction. A 2025 evidence-based PBM consensus guideline concluded PBM is safe for adults and that red-light PBM does not induce DNA damage, while focusing efficacy endorsements mainly on medical indications (neuropathy, wounds, alopecia, radiation dermatitis) rather than “brightening” claims [[46]](https://escholarship.org/uc/item/68j64451).

Because these mechanisms differ, it’s expected that:
- **Ablative fractional resurfacing** produces the strongest single-course improvements in texture/wrinkles but with more downtime and PIH risk in higher Fitzpatrick types.
- **Non-ablative fractional** improves texture/pigment more gently across multiple sessions.
- **Pigment-targeting nanosecond/picosecond lasers** can clear discrete pigment but may trigger PIH—especially at shorter wavelengths—unless delivery is fractionated and parameters are conservative.
- **IPL** is powerful for combined dyschromia + vascular photodamage but is operator- and phototype-sensitive.
- **LED/PBM** is plausibly helpful as an adjunct (inflammation/vascular modulation, recovery support) but the most rigorous evidence base is not in “skin whitening”.

---

## 2) Conditions and outcomes: photoaging vs “brightening/whitening” vs hyperpigmentation

### Photoaging (photodamage) targets
Typical targets: **fine/coarse wrinkles, rough texture, enlarged pores, laxity, dyspigmentation, telangiectasia/erythema** [[5]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/surgery/articles/10.3389/fsurg.2023.1076848/full).

**Outcome tools** in recent clinical studies include:
- Standardized photo scales (e.g., global photoaging scores, GAIS, Fitzpatrick Wrinkle Score) [[4]](https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s13555-025-01404-3), [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10194056/)
- Imaging platforms such as **VISIA** for wrinkles/pores/spots/texture [[5]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/surgery/articles/10.3389/fsurg.2023.1076848/full), [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12210001/)
- Biophysical indices (melanin index, erythema index, elasticity, hydration) [[5]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/surgery/articles/10.3389/fsurg.2023.1076848/full)
- Ultrasound measures (epidermal thickness, dermal density) [[5]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/surgery/articles/10.3389/fsurg.2023.1076848/full)
- Reflectance confocal microscopy and histology for remodeling signals [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12210001/)

### “Skin whitening/brightening”
In clinical science, “brightening” should be separated into:
- **Lesion-level pigment reduction** (lentigines, melasma patches, PIH macules)
- **Whole-face tone uniformity** (reduction in contrast between lesional and surrounding skin, and/or reduced redness)

Objective tone/brightness methods increasingly used in pigment research include **CIE L\*a\*b\*** colorimetry and **ITA°** (Individual Typology Angle), plus **ΔE** color difference (tone uniformity) [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12475913/). Notably, in melasma, visible-light protection can improve objective tone uniformity even when clinical severity indices improve similarly (more on this in §8) [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12475913/).

### Hyperpigmentation categories
- **Solar lentigines (“age spots”) / freckles**: discrete epidermal pigment targets; tend to respond well to pigment-targeting lasers or IPL.
- **Melasma**: chronic relapsing disorder with pigment + inflammatory + vascular + photoaging-like dermal changes; high recurrence risk; device therapy is controversial and often adjunctive [[92]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11148313/), [[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12795099/).
- **PIH**: complication risk after procedures; strongly associated with higher Fitzpatrick types and with procedural inflammation.

---

## 3) Current landscape (2022–Feb 2026) of light-based modalities used for photoaging and dyschromia

### A) Ablative lasers (resurfacing)
- **Fractional CO₂ (10,600 nm)**: widely positioned as a cornerstone resurfacing modality; a 2024 international modified‑Delphi consensus described fractional ablative CO₂ laser resurfacing as a “gold standard for nonsurgical skin rejuvenation,” while emphasizing systems vary and “not all CO₂ lasers are equal” [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).
- **Er:YAG (2,940 nm)**: more superficial water absorption peak; tends to offer faster recovery than CO₂ at the expense of less coagulative tightening (commonly cited mechanistic/clinical trade-off) [[4]](https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s13555-025-01404-3).
- **Hybrid ablative approaches**: combine ablative + non-ablative wavelengths to balance downtime and efficacy (e.g., CO₂ + 1570 nm) [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10194056/).

### B) Non-ablative fractional lasers (NAFL)
- **1550–1565 nm (erbium glass/diode family)**: dermal remodeling, texture and wrinkles; multiple sessions.
- **1927 nm thulium fractional**: more superficial epidermal/superficial dermal targeting; used for dyschromia and superficial photodamage. Mechanistically sits between classic non-ablative (1550) and ablative wavelengths, and can range from non-ablative to more ablative-like effects depending on settings and tip design [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12264580/).

### C) Pigment-targeting nanosecond and picosecond lasers
- **Q-switched (nanosecond) 532/694/755/1064 nm**: pigment fragmentation; higher PIH risk especially at shorter wavelengths and in darker phototypes.
- **Picosecond lasers**: more photoacoustic effect; increasingly combined with **fractional optical delivery** (MLA/DLA/holographic arrays) producing micro-injury patterns (LIOB/LIC) aimed at remodeling with reduced thermal diffusion [[12]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-022-03704-y).

### D) IPL (intense pulsed light)
Used for **photorejuvenation** (pigment + vascular), erythema/telangiectasia, and selected pigment disorders. Evidence in 2024 rosacea meta-analysis supports meaningful erythema/telangiectasia improvement but highlights broad parameter heterogeneity and variable study quality [[44]](https://ijdvl.com/content/126/2024/0/1/pdf/IJDVL_1029_2022.pdf). Safety is highly operator- and phototype-dependent [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12433457/).

### E) LED / PBM (photobiomodulation)
PBM is widely used commercially for “rejuvenation” and post-procedure recovery. A 2025 evidence-based consensus guideline supports safety (adults; red light PBM no DNA damage), with strongest efficacy endorsements for medical indications rather than cosmetic brightening [[46]](https://escholarship.org/uc/item/68j64451). Amber (≈590 nm) PBM is being explored for melasma via vascular/angiogenesis pathways (VEGF/SCF) [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36552713/), but controlled clinical outcomes remain mixed/limited [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04567-9).

---

## 4) Photoaging: clinical applications and recent evidence by modality

### 4.1 Fractional ablative CO₂ (10,600 nm): still the benchmark for texture + wrinkles
**Why it works:** Water absorption → controlled ablation + coagulation columns → robust collagen remodeling and resurfacing.

**Consensus/real-world practice (2024):**
- 2024 international modified‑Delphi consensus frames fractional ablative CO₂ as a “gold standard” for rejuvenation and provides practical patterns for contraindications, prophylaxis, anesthesia, PIH mitigation, and adjunctive treatments [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).
- Common contraindications endorsed by the panel included active facial infections (95%); pregnancy/breastfeeding, recent sun exposure, and burns were also frequently considered contraindications [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).
- PIH mitigation: many panelists recommend topical pretreatment beginning ~4 weeks before and stopping shortly before treatment [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).
- HSV prophylaxis patterns were common; a consensus regimen cited was valacyclovir 500 mg for 5–7 days starting the day before or day of treatment [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).

**Recent parameter-focused trial evidence (skin-of-color relevance):**
- A 2025 randomized split-face clinical trial in a Chinese cohort examined fractional CO₂ settings for **static periorbital wrinkles** and PIH mitigation. It found CO₂ AFL improved wrinkles, and that **low energy + high density** (10–12.5 mJ, 15% density) showed the strongest wrinkle-score reductions at 3 and 6 months. A hydroquinone regimen significantly reduced PIH (p=0.02) [[4]](https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s13555-025-01404-3).  
  *Implication:* In PIH-prone populations, “gentler energy, more fractional coverage” plus pigment-suppressive topical strategy may outperform “higher energy, lower density” for some indications.

**Hybrid CO₂ + non-ablative wavelength (downtime-optimized resurfacing):**
- A 2023 retrospective study of a **hybrid fractional device** combining CO₂ (10,600 nm) with 1570 nm (non-ablative) for periorbital rejuvenation reported statistically significant improvements across multiple wrinkle/hollow scales with **mean downtime ~5.9 days** and erythema resolution ~18 days; hyperpigmentation occurred but resolved within ~30 days in reported cases [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10194056/).  
  *Interpretation:* Hybrid devices represent a “middle path” between aggressive CO₂ resurfacing and gentler NAFL—still requiring downtime and erythema tolerance.

**How ablative CO₂ compares to non-ablative (meta-analytic view):**
- A 2022 systematic review/meta-analysis of RCTs (search to 2020) comparing ablative vs non-ablative lasers for rejuvenation found **no statistically significant differences** in odds of “excellent/good/fair improvement” or side effects in pooled analyses, while emphasizing small sample sizes and need for higher-quality RCTs [[3]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35107665/).  
  *Practical takeaway:* In modern practice, the choice is often driven less by “can it work?” and more by **downtime tolerance, risk profile (PIH), and the specific dominant problem** (deep rhytids vs dyschromia vs pores).

---

### 4.2 Non-ablative fractional lasers (1550–1565 nm; 1927 nm thulium): “workhorse” multi-session rejuvenation

#### 1927 nm fractional thulium (photoaging + dyschromia-leaning)
- A 2023 prospective study in Asian subjects (Fitzpatrick II–IV) receiving **three monthly full-face treatments** with a 1927 nm fractional thulium fiber laser reported significant improvements in **wrinkles**, **pores**, **melanin index**, and **elasticity**, plus a small but significant increase in epidermal thickness; erythema index did not significantly improve. No PIH changes or adverse events were observed in this cohort/protocol [[5]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/surgery/articles/10.3389/fsurg.2023.1076848/full).  
  *Clinical pattern:* 1927 nm tends to be positioned for **superficial photodamage and tone** with relatively lower downtime than ablative resurfacing (though real-world PIH risk still depends on settings and phototype).

#### 1550 vs 1565 nm NAFL (periorbital photoaging)
- A 2025 randomized split-face trial comparing 1550 and 1565 nm NAFL for periorbital photodamage in Asians found both wavelengths effective and safe, with **better, more consistent improvements in patients ≥45** and no significant difference between wavelengths [[8]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40064643/).  
  *Clinical implication:* Patient age/degree of photodamage may predict response more than small wavelength differences within this band.

#### Pores and texture: NAFL vs picosecond fractional optics
- A 2024 randomized split-face trial found both **1064 nm picosecond with microlens array** and **1565 nm NAFL** reduced enlarged pore counts through 2-month follow-up; pain was lower with the picosecond MLA side and pigmentation occurrence was not significantly different between devices in that dataset [[9]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38396012/).

---

### 4.3 Picosecond fractional and “fractional-like” approaches: remodeling with less thermal diffusion
**Mechanistic advance:** Fractional optical delivery (MLA/DLA/holographic arrays) creates patterned high-fluence microspots inducing **laser-induced optical breakdown (LIOB)** and cavitation, stimulating regeneration/remodeling while sparing surrounding tissue [[12]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-022-03704-y).

**Photoaging comparative evidence (2025):**
- A 2025 split-face study compared fractional 1064 nm picosecond (Resolve/PicoWay) vs fractional Q-switched 1064 nm Nd:YAG for photoaging. Both sides improved wrinkles/pores/texture on VISIA, showed RCM score decreases, and histology suggested increased collagen and improved elastic fiber alignment, with no significant between-device differences in most efficacy outcomes; pain was higher with the picosecond fractional side [[11]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12210001/).  
  *Clinical reality:* Picosecond fractional can be effective for texture/photoaging, but “more advanced” does not automatically mean “less painful.”

---

### 4.4 IPL for photoaging (“photorejuvenation”)
**Where IPL is strongest:** Combined **erythema/telangiectasia + mottled pigmentation** patterns typical of photodamage; “brighter” appearance may often come from **redness reduction** as much as pigment reduction.

**Evidence base (quality caveats):**
- A 2024 systematic review/meta-analysis in rosacea (a vascular redness condition frequently treated with IPL) found consistent reductions in erythema/telangiectasia and symptoms across studies, but also emphasized wide parameter variation, frequent lack of control groups, and risk-of-bias limitations [[44]](https://ijdvl.com/content/126/2024/0/1/pdf/IJDVL_1029_2022.pdf).  
- A pre-2022 (2021) systematic review of IPL in skin rejuvenation concluded IPL is effective across many photoaging signs but highlighted low methodological quality in many studies and lack of consensus on parameters [[53]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34609598/).  
  *Interpretation for 2022–2026 practice:* IPL remains widely used and often effective for “photorejuvenation,” but outcomes are highly dependent on **operator skill, pulse stacking strategy, cut-off filters, cooling, and phototype selection**, and the literature is heterogeneous.

---

### 4.5 LED/PBM for photoaging
- The 2025 PBM evidence-based consensus guideline primarily supports PBM safety and identifies strong evidence for medical indications; aesthetic endpoints are not the best-established part of the evidence base in that guideline [[46]](https://escholarship.org/uc/item/68j64451).  
- Some trials exist (e.g., reported periocular wrinkle volume reductions in PBM journal titles), but detailed, reproducible 2022–2026 RCT parameter/outcome reporting was not available in the retrieved source excerpts here, so strong quantitative claims about wrinkle reduction from LED masks should be treated cautiously [[46]](https://escholarship.org/uc/item/68j64451), [[59]](https://journals.sagepub.com/doi/10.1089/photob.2022.0114), [[60]](https://clinicaltrials.gov/study/NCT04911140).

---

## 5) Hyperpigmentation & “brightening”: what’s proven vs what’s controversial

### 5.1 Solar lentigines (age spots) / freckles: high response, but PIH risk in darker phototypes

**Comparative RCT evidence (2025):**
- A 2025 randomized trial comparing **730 nm picosecond**, **532 nm picosecond**, and **694 nm Q-switched ruby** on randomized facial areas found all three improved clearance and ITA° measures, with important differences in pain and healing times; histology suggested least basal vacuolar degeneration with 730 nm picosecond and most extensive injury with QS ruby [[30]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40616673/).  
  *Interpretation:* Multiple laser types can clear lentigines, but tissue-injury profiles differ—relevant to PIH risk and downtime counseling.

**Key recent advancement: fractionated 532 nm picosecond delivery to reduce PIH**
- A 2024 prospective randomized self-controlled study in Asians with Fitzpatrick III–V compared conventional 532 nm picosecond treatment vs a **microlens array (MLA) “fractional pigment toning”** approach. Clearance improved in both, but PIH incidence was dramatically lower with MLA fractionation across follow-up (e.g., 2 weeks: 64% vs 8%; 3 months: 96% vs 36%; 6 months: 88% vs 16%) [[31]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39075754/).  
  *Clinical significance:* This is one of the clearest recent demonstrations that **beam fractionation/optical delivery design** can change the safety profile (PIH) while maintaining pigment clearance—especially important for darker phototypes.

**Systematic review context (2025):**
- A 2025 systematic review of solar lentigines trials reported broad success ranges across modalities (IPL and picosecond among higher ranges) and noted IPL/PDL were less associated with PIH than some other approaches, while calling for larger RCTs and noting benefit from combining lasers with topical agents to reduce PIH [[33]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40145274/).  
  *Caution:* The wide success ranges reflect heterogeneity in lesion selection, endpoints, settings, and follow-up.

---

### 5.2 Melasma: the hard problem (chronic, relapsing; device use is debated)

#### Why melasma is not just “too much pigment”
Modern reviews emphasize melasma overlaps with photoaging biology: basement membrane disruption, solar elastosis, angiogenesis, and inflammatory/mast cell infiltration—so durable “brightening” usually requires more than pigment fragmentation [[92]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11148313/).

#### The relapse driver many patients underestimate: visible light
Visible light (especially blue-violet) can induce pigmentation and worsen melasma relapses; iron oxide–tinted sunscreens reduce visible light transmission and improve clinical outcomes in melasma and other hyperpigmentation disorders [[49]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13555-025-01642-5), [[104]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0190962221029819). (More in §8.)

---

#### 5.2.1 Low-fluence Q-switched 1064 nm Nd:YAG (“laser toning”): mixed evidence
- A 2022 systematic review/meta-analysis found **no significant MASI difference** between low-fluence QS 1064 and drug groups, while combination therapy (laser + drugs) outperformed laser alone; it also found adding LQSNY to IPL improved outcomes vs IPL alone in pooled data [[24]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35621242/).  
  *Implication:* “Laser toning” may be better viewed as an adjunct or alternative when topicals are limited, rather than a superior monotherapy.

- A placebo-controlled RCT record (abstract-level) reported that when combined with exfoliation, hydroquinone 4% and sunscreen, low-fluence QS Nd:YAG did **not add significant benefit** beyond the topical regimen in that protocol [[107]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40932145/).  
  *Meaning:* Some improvements attributed to devices may actually come from the co-interventions (topicals + strict photoprotection).

---

#### 5.2.2 Picosecond lasers for melasma: trend toward 1064 nm and toward fractionated delivery
**Head-to-head RCT (2023; Fitzpatrick III–IV):**
- A 2023 randomized assessor-blinded trial compared **non-fractional 1064 nm picosecond Nd:YAG**, **non-fractional 755 nm picosecond alexandrite**, and **2% hydroquinone** for melasma. All groups improved, but 1064 ps Nd:YAG produced the greatest MASI reduction and was superior to 755 ps alexandrite and 2% HQ in this study; recurrence occurred in a minority (6.8%) [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/medicine/articles/10.3389/fmed.2023.1132823/full).  
  *Practical take:* This supports the clinical migration toward **1064 nm** (deeper, less epidermal melanin competition) for melasma in darker Asian phototypes.

**Meta-analysis synthesis (2023):**
- A 2023 systematic review/meta-analysis of RCTs concluded picosecond lasers reduce MASI/mMASI overall, with subgroup analysis supporting **1064 nm picosecond** as effective without significant side effects, while **755 nm picosecond** was not significantly better than topical agents and was reported to cause PIH in included studies [[26]](https://link.springer.com/doi/10.1007/s10103-023-03744-y).  
  *Interpretation:* Wavelength selection matters; “picosecond” alone is not a guarantee of safety in melasma.

**Split-face evidence for fractional optics to reduce recurrence (2025):**
- A 2025 randomized split-face study compared low-fluence 1064 ps Nd:YAG **with vs without** microlens array. MASI improved on both sides; importantly, **6‑month recurrence** was lower on MLA side (1 vs 6 patients) [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04655-w).  
  *Clinical significance:* Suggests a plausible role for **fractional optical delivery** not only for safety, but for relapse modulation—potentially by reducing inflammation/thermal burden.

---

#### 5.2.3 1927 nm thulium fractional laser and laser-assisted drug delivery (LADD) with tranexamic acid (TXA)
**Mechanistic plausibility:**
- 1927 nm fractional thulium can range from non-ablative-like epidermal MTZs to more ablative superficial effects depending on pulse energy and tip; this “tunable superficiality” is relevant when treating epidermal pigment while trying to avoid inflammatory depth that drives PIH [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12264580/).

**Split-face double-blind RCT pilot (FTL + topical TXA):**
- A split-face, double-blind randomized pilot studied fractional 1927 nm thulium laser with immediate post-laser **topical TXA** vs control application after each session (weekly ×4) in Fitzpatrick III–V recalcitrant melasma. Both sides improved; differences between TXA vs control were limited at key timepoints, and no serious adverse events were reported [[85]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32506227/).  
  *Clinical reading:* Fractional thulium may improve melasma, but the incremental benefit of topical TXA delivery over laser alone remains uncertain and may be modest in some protocols.

**CO₂ fractional LADD comparisons (2025):**
- A 2025 split-face comparative study evaluated fractional CO₂ followed by transepidermal delivery of **10% TXA vs 20% ascorbic acid** over 6 monthly sessions; it concluded both were useful with results more favorable toward TXA, while calling for larger controlled trials [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12170786/).  
  *Caveat:* Baseline vascularity imbalance existed between sides in that study [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12170786/).

---

#### 5.2.4 IPL for melasma: used, but relapse/PIH risk makes protocol design critical
- A 2022 case series using IPL with “advanced optimal pulse technology” reported mMASI improvements over an 8‑month course and no relapse at 4 months post-course in 10 patients, using higher cutoff wavelength (640 nm) and pulse-width/energy adjustments intended to reduce epidermal irritation [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9351870/).  
  *Evidence caution:* Small uncontrolled design; no objective instrumentation; nevertheless provides insight into how clinicians attempt to reduce relapse risk via longer cutoff filters and gentler pulse strategies [[35]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9351870/).

- Broadly, a 2026 systematic review/meta-analysis of **laser/light + topical agents** for melasma found combination therapy produced significant improvements by 8–16 weeks (not at 4 weeks), but with substantially higher adverse event risk (erythema/PIH), and rated safety evidence certainty very low [[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12795099/).  
  *Clinical implication:* Combination regimens can work, but often at the cost of more irritation—exactly what triggers PIH/relapse in melasma-prone patients.

---

### 5.3 Post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation (PIH): the key limiting factor for “brightening” in darker phototypes
- A 2025 systematic review on PIH prevention in skin of color (Fitzpatrick III–VI) found laser therapy precipitated >95% of PIH cases included; fractional CO₂ was the most common precipitating laser type in their dataset. Preventive measures varied; **only sunscreen consistently prevented PIH incidence** in the included studies, while topical corticosteroids and systemic TXA were less successful, and cooling air devices were reported to exacerbate PIH development in those datasets [[65]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12062726/).  
  *Practical take:* PIH prevention is still more reliably photoprotection-driven than “magic adjunct” driven.

---

## 6) IPL vs lasers vs LED for “brightening”: what each realistically delivers

### IPL
**Best for:** “photorejuvenation” where the perceived “brightness” is from improved **redness + mottled pigment**. Strongly operator-dependent; riskier in darker phototypes due to competing epidermal melanin absorption [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12433457/).  
**Evidence base:** meaningful improvements shown in redness conditions (rosacea) but heterogeneous parameters and study designs [[44]](https://ijdvl.com/content/126/2024/0/1/pdf/IJDVL_1029_2022.pdf).

### Lasers
- **Discrete pigment lesions (lentigines):** very effective, but PIH risk is a key limiter; picosecond with fractionated delivery is a major recent safety advance [[31]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39075754/), [[30]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40616673/).
- **Melasma:** can improve MASI, but recurrence is common; safest trend favors 1064 nm picosecond and conservative/fractional delivery approaches [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/medicine/articles/10.3389/fmed.2023.1132823/full), [[26]](https://link.springer.com/doi/10.1007/s10103-023-03744-y), [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04655-w).

### LED/PBM
**Most defensible role today:** adjunct for inflammation/vascular modulation and recovery; emerging role in melasma is plausible but not yet consistently proven as a standalone “brightening” modality. Mechanistic work suggests 590 nm LED can downregulate VEGF/SCF in endothelial cells and may improve melasma-associated erythema/pigmentation in pilot clinical observations [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36552713/), but a 12-week pilot RCT comparing amber PBM to topical TXA found no significant MASI/PGA improvements (underpowered, early termination) [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04567-9).  
**Safety:** PBM is generally considered safe in adults in the 2025 consensus guideline [[46]](https://escholarship.org/uc/item/68j64451).

---

## 7) Safety, adverse effects, and best-practice risk control (especially Fitzpatrick IV–VI)

### 7.1 The big risks across modalities
- **PIH / hypopigmentation** (especially Fitzpatrick III–VI; melasma-prone)
- **Thermal burns/blistering/scarring** (especially IPL when misused)
- **Prolonged erythema** (especially resurfacing)
- **HSV reactivation** (resurfacing)
- **Ocular injury** (periorbital treatments; home devices; inadequate eyewear)
- **Laser plume exposure** (ablative resurfacing)

---

### 7.2 IPL safety: burns and pigmentary injury are real
A 2025 case report described **second-degree burns** in a Fitzpatrick IV patient after IPL at a non-medical spa, emphasizing lack of individualized settings and dismissal of pain complaints [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12433457/). The report also summarized evidence that IPL adverse reactions can be common (erythema, hyperpigmentation, purpura, blisters, hypopigmentation) and that darker pigmentation/higher fluence correlate with more severe side effects [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12433457/).  
**Clinical guardrails:**
- Conservative settings for higher phototypes; higher cutoff filters; epidermal cooling; test spots; strict “stop if pain/overheating” discipline [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12433457/).
- Operator training and medical oversight matter; complication series link many adverse outcomes to excessive energy and treating darker/tanned skin without appropriate adjustments [[61]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12433457/).

---

### 7.3 PIH prevention: what the best synthesis says
The 2025 PIH prevention systematic review in skin of color concluded that **sunscreen** was the only preventive measure that consistently prevented PIH incidence in included studies, while other measures were less reliable; it also reported some cooling air device use exacerbated PIH development in included datasets [[65]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12062726/).  
**Practical application:** For any “brightening” plan in Fitzpatrick IV–VI or melasma-prone patients, prevention is dominated by:
- **Aggressive photoprotection**, and
- **Minimizing procedure-induced inflammation** (lower fluence/density adjustments, fractionated delivery, fewer passes, longer intervals, careful endpoints).

---

### 7.4 Ablative fractional CO₂ safety playbook (consensus patterns)
The 2024 CO₂ fractional ablative consensus reported common practices including antiviral prophylaxis, topical anesthesia, and individualized setting adjustments for Fitzpatrick III–IV, and noted many clinicians use pre-procedure topical regimens to reduce PIH risk [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).  
Hybrid fractional devices can shorten downtime relative to traditional approaches but still produce erythema and transient pigment change in some patients [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10194056/).

---

### 7.5 Ocular safety: underemphasized but potentially catastrophic
Key points from modern laser safety literature:
- Blink reflex is too slow to protect against dermatologic lasers, especially Q-switched/picosecond pulses; the retina is at highest risk for 400–1400 nm (“retinal hazard zone”) [[67]](https://cosmoderma.org/content/130/2022/2/1/pdf/CSDM-2-24.pdf).
- Q-switched/picosecond pulses can cause photoacoustic damage; near-infrared beams can be invisible and especially dangerous [[67]](https://cosmoderma.org/content/130/2022/2/1/pdf/CSDM-2-24.pdf), [[69]](https://www.aslms.org/docs/default-source/for-professionals/mentorship-program/alsms-laser-safety-guide.pdf).
- Reviews highlight that many ocular injuries are preventable with proper protection; lack of enforced regulation and lack of mandatory adverse-event reporting contribute to ongoing risk [[68]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10616986/).
- A 2024 review focusing on office-based and home-use devices found that most IPL ocular injuries were linked to improper eyewear; iris atrophy was commonly permanent in reported cases [[71]](https://www.dovepress.com/is-it-possible-for-light-based-hair-removal-home-devices-to-induce-ocu-peer-reviewed-fulltext-article-CCID).

**Best practice essentials:**
- Correct wavelength-rated eyewear for everyone in the room; consider multi-wavelength emission (e.g., 532 devices may also emit 1064) when selecting eyewear [[67]](https://cosmoderma.org/content/130/2022/2/1/pdf/CSDM-2-24.pdf).
- Use **metal corneal shields** when treating periocular areas; avoid plastic shields that can melt [[69]](https://www.aslms.org/docs/default-source/for-professionals/mentorship-program/alsms-laser-safety-guide.pdf).
- Avoid cornea-toxic prep solutions near eyes (ASLMS guide notes chlorhexidine can cause severe keratitis; povidone‑iodine is safer) [[69]](https://www.aslms.org/docs/default-source/for-professionals/mentorship-program/alsms-laser-safety-guide.pdf).

---

### 7.6 Plume/smoke safety (ablative resurfacing)
The ASLMS safety guide details that ablative laser plume contains ultrafine particles and potentially toxic chemicals and viable pathogens; effective control requires local exhaust ventilation and high-efficiency filtration (e.g., ULPA), with the suction nozzle close to the source [[69]](https://www.aslms.org/docs/default-source/for-professionals/mentorship-program/alsms-laser-safety-guide.pdf). ASLMS position statements endorse plume evacuation wherever tissue is vaporized/ejected [[70]](https://www.aslms.org/for-professionals/professional-resources/safety-and-complications/aslms-laser-and-energy-device-plume-position-statement).

---

## 8) The most overlooked “advancement” for brightening outcomes: visible-light photoprotection

A recurring finding across pigment science is that **treatments fail when visible light exposure isn’t controlled**, particularly for melasma.

- Visible light comprises ~half of solar radiation and can cause pigment darkening in Fitzpatrick IV–VI; blue-violet (~415 nm) is particularly pigment-inducing in darker skin types [[104]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0190962221029819).
- A 2026 review notes high-energy visible light contributes to persistent pigmentation and photoaging via oxidative stress and opsin‑3 mediated melanogenesis; tinted sunscreens with iron oxides/pigmentary TiO₂ are among the most effective available visible-light protection strategies [[49]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13555-025-01642-5).
- Critically, a 2025 randomized investigator‑blinded melasma sunscreen trial comparing **tinted (iron oxides + pigmentary TiO₂)** vs **untinted** sunscreen found both prevented darkening and reduced clinical mMASI, but the tinted sunscreen produced a **significantly greater improvement in objective pigmentation uniformity** between melasma lesions and surrounding skin (via L\*, ITA°, ΔE differences) [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12475913/).  
  *Translation to practice:* If a clinic sells “brightening laser packages” without a visible-light photoprotection plan, durability and relapse prevention are structurally undermined.

---

## 9) Recent scientific/technical advancements changing practice (2022–Feb 2026)

### 9.1 Fractional optical delivery (MLA/DLA/holographic arrays) for pigment safety
- For lentigines in darker phototypes, MLA fractionation of 532 nm picosecond treatment dramatically reduced PIH while maintaining clearance—one of the most clinically meaningful recent advances for safe pigment work in Fitzpatrick III–V [[31]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39075754/).
- For melasma, MLA use with low-fluence 1064 ps Nd:YAG reduced recurrence at 6 months vs non-MLA treatment in a split-face study [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04655-w).
- Fractional picosecond delivery mechanisms and LIOB/LIC remodeling are summarized in a 2023 review detailing commercial array types and mechanisms [[12]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-022-03704-y).

### 9.2 Hybrid fractional resurfacing (ablative + non-ablative in one treatment)
Hybrid CO₂ + 1570 nm periorbital rejuvenation data show meaningful improvements with manageable downtime/erythema windows and generally transient adverse effects, representing continued device evolution toward “less downtime, still strong results” [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10194056/).

### 9.3 Parameter optimization for skin of color
The 2025 fractional CO₂ periorbital RCT suggests **low energy + higher density** can be superior for wrinkle outcomes and PIH mitigation when paired with pigment-suppressive topical strategy (hydroquinone) in a Chinese population [[4]](https://link.springer.com/10.1007/s13555-025-01404-3). This supports the broader trend: in PIH-prone patients, “more aggressive” is not necessarily better.

### 9.4 Laser-assisted drug delivery (LADD) and multimodal regimens
- Fractional thulium–assisted TXA delivery has been evaluated in split-face randomized pilot work, showing improvements but mixed incremental benefit [[85]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32506227/).
- Fractional CO₂ LADD with TXA vs vitamin C suggests TXA may be more favorable, but larger controlled trials are needed [[87]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12170786/).
- A 2026 meta-analysis suggests laser/light + topical combinations improve melasma outcomes by 8–16 weeks but increase adverse event risk substantially (erythema/PIH) [[52]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12795099/).  
  *Interpretation:* Multimodal regimens can be “more effective but less forgiving,” requiring careful sequencing and downtime planning.

---

## 10) Practical, evidence-aligned “selection logic” (how clinicians typically match device to problem)

### Photoaging-dominant (wrinkles/texture, significant photodamage)
- **Fractional ablative CO₂**: strongest single-course resurfacing; higher downtime/erythema and higher PIH management burden; consensus recognizes it as a leading modality [[1]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11776446/).
- **Hybrid CO₂ + 1570**: intermediate downtime; periorbital improvements shown with ~6 days downtime in one retrospective series [[14]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10194056/).
- **1550/1565 NAFL**: multi-session; better for patients needing minimal downtime; age/degree of photodamage influences results [[8]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40064643/).
- **1927 thulium**: useful for superficial photodamage + pigment; prospective Asian study shows wrinkle/pore and melanin index improvements with no PIH in that cohort [[5]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/surgery/articles/10.3389/fsurg.2023.1076848/full).

### Lentigines / freckles (discrete spots)
- **IPL** or pigment lasers depending on phototype and lesion characteristics; IPL may have lower PIH association in some lentigines literature synthesis but depends on patient/device/operator [[33]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40145274/).
- **Picosecond lasers**: effective; newer wavelengths (e.g., 730 nm) may show less basal injury histologically than some traditional options [[30]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40616673/).
- In Fitzpatrick III–V, **fractionated (MLA) 532 ps** is a compelling safety-forward approach given dramatic PIH reduction vs conventional technique [[31]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/39075754/).

### Melasma (patchy, relapsing)
- First-line foundation: **visible-light + UVA photoprotection** (tinted iron oxide) + topicals; device therapy is adjunctive and relapse-prone [[92]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11148313/), [[49]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s13555-025-01642-5), [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12475913/).
- **1064 nm picosecond Nd:YAG** has supportive RCT and meta-analytic evidence and tends to be safer than shorter wavelengths; fractional optics may reduce recurrence [[25]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/medicine/articles/10.3389/fmed.2023.1132823/full), [[26]](https://link.springer.com/doi/10.1007/s10103-023-03744-y), [[28]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04655-w).
- **Low-fluence QS 1064 “toning”**: evidence suggests it is not consistently superior to drugs as monotherapy; better considered as adjunct in selected patients [[24]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/35621242/), [[107]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/40932145/).
- **1927 nm thulium fractional**: used for epidermal/superficial components and LADD strategies; incremental benefit of TXA delivery remains uncertain and protocol-dependent [[85]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/32506227/), [[34]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12264580/).

---

## 11) Key debates and open problems (where 2022–2026 literature still doesn’t fully settle questions)

1) **Durability / maintenance standards**  
Most device studies have follow-ups measured in months, while melasma can persist 10–20 years with high relapse rates; maintenance protocols and long-term relapse prevention remain insufficiently standardized [[103]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC9790748/), [[29]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12696807/).

2) **Best endpoints for “brightening”**  
Clinical scores (MASI/mMASI) may improve without equal improvements in objective uniformity; objective L\*/ITA/ΔE metrics can capture “brightness/tone uniformity” more directly, as shown in sunscreen RCT work [[102]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12475913/). Device studies still often prioritize clinician scales over colorimetry.

3) **Skin-of-color safety evidence gaps**  
PIH prevention review data are heavily Asian-population dominated in included prevention studies, limiting generalizability to all Fitzpatrick V–VI populations globally [[65]](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC12062726/).

4) **Parameter heterogeneity and reproducibility**  
IPL studies show extreme variation in wavelength bands, pulse durations, and fluences, which makes cross-study comparisons and standardized counseling difficult [[44]](https://ijdvl.com/content/126/2024/0/1/pdf/IJDVL_1029_2022.pdf), [[53]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/34609598/).

5) **LED/PBM for pigment**  
Mechanistic plausibility is strong (vascular and melanogenesis pathway modulation), but controlled trials remain limited and sometimes negative/underpowered; optimal wavelength/dose schedules for melasma are not established [[56]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36552713/), [[58]](https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10103-025-04567-9), [[108]](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/38018017/).

---
